headline: A Billion People Gained Clean Water: The SDG Story So Far
meta_description: Nearly a billion people now have safe drinking water. The UN's latest report shows real wins—and urgent work ahead before 2030.
summary_short: Since 2015, nearly one billion people gained access to safe drinking water and 1.2 billion to sanitation. Yet 36% of targets remain off track.
body:
When the United Nations set the Sustainable Development Goals in 2015, it was an audacious promise: end poverty, ensure clean water, tackle climate change—by 2030. With fewer than five years remaining, a new UN report shows the world has actually delivered on some fronts.

Nearly one billion people now have safe drinking water where they didn't before. Another 1.2 billion gained access to safely managed sanitation. HIV infections fell 30 percent. Electricity now reaches 92 percent of the global population. The internet surged from serving 40 percent to 74 percent of people worldwide.

Yet the report, released this week alongside the UN's High-Level Political Forum in New York, reveals a sobering reality: most goals still lag far behind schedule. Of 139 targets with available data, only 36 percent are on track or making moderate progress. Nearly half are advancing too slowly, while 15 percent have actually regressed.

The gaps remain stark. One in ten people still live in extreme poverty. Food insecurity affects 2.3 billion. Maternal mortality sits nearly three times above target. Escalating conflicts, climate change, and slowing economic growth have stalled momentum, hitting the world's most vulnerable hardest.

Yet the goals persist—not as finished work, but as an unfinished blueprint demanding urgent action.
